preamble.summary: A prayer of David that functions as a legal appeal to God as judge. The psalmist opens by asserting his innocence — his lips, his steps, his heart have been tested and found without deceit — and then petitions God for protection from deadly enemies who are closing in. The imagery builds from legal defense to military siege to predatory lions. The psalm reaches its climax in the final verse with one of the most intimate statements of hope in the Psalter: 'As for me, I will behold your face in righteousness; when I awake, I will be satisfied with your likeness.'
preamble.remarkable: The psalm's final verse (v. 15) stands in deliberate contrast to verse 14, which describes the wicked satisfied with earthly abundance — their bellies filled, their children inheriting their surplus. Against this picture of material satiety, the psalmist offers a radically different definition of satisfaction: 'I will be satisfied with your likeness' (esbe'ah be-haqits temunatekha). The word temunah ('form, likeness, appearance') is the same word used in Numbers 12:8, where God says Moses beholds 'the form of the LORD.' The psalmist's ultimate satisfaction is not in possessions, descendants, or long life but in seeing God. This verse, together with Psalm 16:11, represents the highest reach of the Psalter's theology of divine presence.
preamble.friction: The psalmist's claims of innocence in verses 3-5 are remarkably strong: 'You have tested my heart... you find nothing... my mouth does not transgress... I have kept from the paths of the violent.' This language troubles readers who expect all biblical figures to acknowledge sinfulness. The innocence claimed here is not absolute moral perfection but covenantal integrity — the psalmist has not committed the specific crimes of which enemies accuse him. This is legal language: I am not guilty of what they charge. The claims should be read as courtroom defense, not as theological assertion of sinlessness.
preamble.connections: The final verse ('I will behold your face in righteousness') directly echoes Psalm 11:7 ('the upright will behold his face') and anticipates the 'beatific vision' theology that develops through the biblical tradition. The phrase be-haqits ('when I awake') connects to Daniel 12:2, where the dead 'awake' to everlasting life. The lion imagery (vv. 11-12) appears also in Psalm 10:9 and 22:13, linking these psalms in their portrait of predatory enemies. The 'apple of the eye' (ishon bat ayin, v. 8) reappears in Deuteronomy 32:10 and Proverbs 7:2.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּפִלָּ֗ה לְדָ֫וִ֥ד שִׁמְעָ֤ה יְהוָ֨ה ׀ צֶ֗דֶק הַקְשִׁ֥יבָה רִנָּתִ֗י הַאֲזִ֥ינָה תְפִלָּתִ֑י בְּ֝לֹ֗א שִׂפְתֵ֥י מִרְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear the right, O LORD, attend unto my cry, give ear unto my prayer, that goeth not out of feigned lips.
verses.rendering:
A prayer of David.
Hear a just cause, LORD; attend to my cry.
Give ear to my prayer —
it comes from lips without deceit.
verses.expanded_rendering: The superscription identifies this as a tefillah ('prayer') — a term used for only five psalms (17, 86, 90, 102, 142). The psalm begins with three imperatives demanding God's attention: shim'ah ('hear!'), haqshivah ('attend, pay close attention!'), ha'azinah ('give ear!'). The first object is tsedeq ('righteousness, justice, a just cause') — the psalmist is not merely praying but presenting a legal case. The final clause establishes credibility: be-lo siftei mirmah ('from lips without deceit'). Before arguing his case, the psalmist asserts that his very act of praying is honest. The lips that speak this prayer are not the smooth, deceptive lips of Psalm 12 but lips free of mirmah ('deceit, fraud, treachery').
translator_notes: The three verbs for hearing (shama, qashav, azan) create a rhetorical escalation: hear, attend, give ear. Each adds urgency. The legal overtone of tsedeq ('a just cause') sets the psalm in the framework of a court appeal — the psalmist is bringing a case before the divine judge.

verses.text_hebrew: מִלְּפָנֶ֥יךָ מִשְׁפָּטִ֥י יֵצֵ֑א עֵ֝ינֶ֗יךָ תֶּחֱזֶ֥ינָה מֵישָׁרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Let my sentence come forth from thy presence; let thine eyes behold the things that are equal.
verses.rendering:
Let my vindication come from your presence;
let your eyes see what is right.
translator_notes: mishpati ('my judgment, my sentence, my vindication') is legal terminology — the verdict the psalmist seeks from God's court. The psalmist is confident: he wants God to judge because he believes God's eyes will see mesharim ('uprightness, equity, what is straight'). He invites divine scrutiny rather than fearing it.

verses.text_hebrew: בָּ֘חַ֤נְתָּ לִבִּ֨י ׀ פָּ֘קַ֤דְתָּ לַּ֗יְלָה צְרַפְתַּ֥נִי בַל־תִּמְצָ֑א זַ֝מֹּתִ֗י בַּל־יַעֲבׇר־פִּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast proved mine heart; thou hast visited me in the night; thou hast tried me, and shalt find nothing; I am purposed that my mouth shall not transgress.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist now submits his past record as evidence. Three verbs of divine examination: bachanta ('you have tested,' from bachan, 'to examine, assay'), paqadta ('you have visited, inspected'), tseraftani ('you have refined me,' from tsaraf, 'to smelt, purify metal'). God has conducted a thorough investigation — by day and by night — using the metalworker's fire to test the psalmist's character. The result: bal timtsa ('you will find nothing') — no impurity, no hidden wrongdoing, no alloy. The psalmist then adds his conscious resolution: zammoti bal ya'avor pi ('I have resolved that my mouth will not transgress'). The verb zamam means 'to plan, purpose, intend' — this is not accidental purity but deliberate discipline of speech.
translator_notes: The nighttime visitation (paqadta lailah) suggests God's examination during the vulnerable hours when conscious defenses are down — in dreams, in the anxious hours of sleeplessness. Even there, God finds nothing. The three-fold testing echoes Psalm 12:6's image of silver refined in a furnace.

verses.text_hebrew: לִפְעֻלּ֣וֹת אָ֭דָם בִּדְבַ֣ר שְׂפָתֶ֑יךָ אֲנִ֥י שָׁ֝מַ֗רְתִּי אׇרְח֥וֹת פָּרִֽיץ׃
verses.text_kjv: Concerning the works of men, by the word of thy lips I have kept me from the paths of the destroyer.
verses.rendering:
As for human deeds — by the word of your lips
I have avoided the paths of the violent.
translator_notes: The psalmist credits God's word (davar sefatekha, 'the word of your lips') as the guide that kept him from the paths of the parits ('violent one, robber, breaker-through'). The contrast is between two kinds of speech: God's word that directs toward righteousness, and the smooth words of the wicked that lead to violence.

verses.text_kjv: Hold up my goings in thy paths, that my footsteps slip not.
verses.rendering:
My steps have held firm to your paths;
my feet have not slipped.
translator_notes: The verb tamakh ('to hold, support, grasp') can be read as either a past declaration ('my steps have held firm') or a petition ('hold up my steps'). The ambiguity allows both: the psalmist affirms past faithfulness and implicitly asks for continued support. The 'paths' (ma'gelot) are God's established tracks — the grooves worn by walking in God's way.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲנִֽי־קְרָאתִ֣יךָ כִֽי־תַעֲנֵ֣נִי אֵ֑ל הַֽט־אׇזְנְךָ֥ לִ֝֗י שְׁמַ֣ע אִמְרָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I have called upon thee, for thou wilt hear me, O God: incline thine ear unto me, and hear my speech.
verses.rendering:
I call on you, for you will answer me, God.
Incline your ear to me; hear my words.
translator_notes: The ki ('for, because') expresses confidence: I call because I know you will answer. The verb anah ('to answer, respond') implies not just hearing but active response. The psalmist's prayer is not cast into a void but directed toward a God with a proven record of answering.

verses.text_hebrew: הַפְלֵ֣ה חֲ֭סָדֶיךָ מוֹשִׁ֣יעַ חוֹסִ֑ים מִ֝מִּתְקוֹמְמִ֗ים בִּימִינֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Shew thy marvellous lovingkindness, O thou that savest by thy right hand them which put their trust in thee from those that rise up against them.
verses.rendering:
Show the wonder of your faithful love,
O savior of those who take refuge
at your right hand from their adversaries.
translator_notes: The verb hafleh ('make wonderful, distinguish') asks for chesed that is not ordinary but spectacular — an act of faithful love so striking it cannot be attributed to coincidence or human effort.

key_terms.semantic_range: your loyal love, your covenant faithfulness, your steadfast kindness
key_terms.note: chesed here is plural (chasadim), suggesting multiple acts or manifestations of faithful love. The psalmist asks God to make these acts wonderful — to demonstrate covenant love in a way that astonishes.

verses.text_hebrew: שׇׁמְרֵ֗נִי כְּאִישׁ֥וֹן בַּת־עָ֑יִן בְּצֵ֥ל כְּ֝נָפֶ֗יךָ תַּסְתִּירֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Keep me as the apple of the eye, hide me under the shadow of thy wings,
verses.rendering:
Guard me like the pupil of the eye;
hide me in the shadow of your wings.
translator_notes: ishon bat ayin ('the little man of the daughter of the eye') is the Hebrew idiom for the pupil. The layered metaphor — a little person reflected inside the eye — suggests that God keeps the psalmist so close that the psalmist is reflected in God's own gaze.

verses.text_hebrew: מִפְּנֵ֣י רְ֭שָׁעִים ז֣וּ שַׁדּ֑וּנִי אֹיְבַ֥י בְּ֝נֶ֗פֶשׁ יַקִּ֥יפוּ עָלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: From the wicked that oppress me, from my deadly enemies, who compass me about.
verses.rendering:
from the wicked who assail me,
from my deadly enemies who surround me.
translator_notes: The phrase oyevai be-nefesh ('my enemies in/against the soul') can mean 'my mortal enemies' (enemies who seek my life) or 'my enemies in soul' (enemies at the deepest level). The verb yaqifu ('they surround, encircle') creates the image of a military siege — the psalmist is hemmed in on all sides.

verses.text_kjv: They are inclosed in their own fat: with their mouth they speak proudly.
verses.rendering:
They close up their callous hearts;
their mouths speak with arrogance.
translator_notes: chelbamo sagru ('their fat they close') — chelev ('fat') metaphorically represents a heart grown insensitive through prosperity and self-indulgence. A fat-enclosed heart cannot feel compassion. The image is of people whose comfort has made them cruel — well-fed, self-satisfied, and indifferent to the suffering they inflict.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֭שֻּׁרֵינוּ עַתָּ֣ה סְבָב֑וּנוּ עֵ֝ינֵיהֶ֗ם יָשִׁ֥יתוּ לִנְט֥וֹת בָּאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: They have now compassed us in our steps: they have set their eyes bowing down to the earth;
verses.rendering:
They track our every step; they surround us.
They fix their eyes to cast us to the ground.
translator_notes: The shift to first person plural ('our steps,' 'surround us') may indicate that the psalmist speaks for a group, or it may be a stylistic variation. The enemies' eyes are set (yashitu) with fixed intent — like a predator locking onto prey before striking. The phrase lintot ba-arets ('to cast down to the earth') describes throwing the victim to the ground.

verses.text_hebrew: דִּמְיֹנ֗וֹ כְּ֭אַרְיֵה יִכְס֣וֹף לִטְר֑וֹף וְ֝כִכְפִ֗יר יֹשֵׁ֥ב בְּמִסְתָּרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Like as a lion that is greedy of his prey, and as it were a young lion lurking in secret places.
verses.rendering:
He is like a lion eager to tear its prey,
like a young lion crouching in ambush.
translator_notes: The lion is the standard metaphor for a powerful, predatory enemy in the Psalms (7:2, 10:9, 22:13, 22:21). The doubling — mature lion and young lion — emphasizes that the threat combines experience and vigor.

verses.text_hebrew: ק֤וּמָה יְהוָ֗ה קַדְּמָ֣ה פָ֭נָיו הַכְרִיעֵ֑הוּ פַּלְּטָ֥ה נַ֝פְשִׁ֗י מֵרָשָׁ֥ע חַרְבֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Arise, O LORD, disappoint him, cast him down: deliver my soul from the wicked, which is thy sword:
verses.rendering:
Rise up, LORD! Confront him; bring him down!
Rescue my soul from the wicked — your sword!
translator_notes: The imperative qumah ('rise up!') echoes Numbers 10:35 and Psalm 12:5 — the war cry for God to take action. The phrase me-rasha charbbekha ('from the wicked — your sword') is ambiguous: is the wicked one God's sword (an instrument of divine testing) or does the psalmist ask to be rescued from the wicked by God's sword? Both readings have support.

verses.text_hebrew: מִמְתִ֥ים יָדְךָ֨ ׀ יְהוָ֡ה מִמְתִ֬ים מֵחֶ֗לֶד חֶלְקָ֤ם בַּחַיִּ֗ים וּצְפוּנְךָ֮ תְּמַלֵּ֢א בִ֫טְנָ֥ם יִשְׂבְּע֥וּ בָנִ֑ים וְהִנִּ֥יחוּ יִ֝תְרָ֗ם לְעוֹלְלֵיהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: From men which are thy hand, O LORD, from men of the world, which have their portion in this life, and whose belly thou fillest with thy hid treasure: they are full of children, and leave the rest of their substance to their babes.
verses.rendering:
From mortals — by your hand, LORD! —
from mortals whose portion is in this life alone.
You fill their bellies with your hidden treasure;
they are satisfied with children
and leave their surplus to their little ones.
translator_notes: The interpretation of this verse is heavily debated. Some read it as describing God's judgment on the wicked (from the dead, by your hand, LORD!). Others read it as describing the wicked's earthly prosperity, which is real but ultimately limited to 'this life.' We follow the second reading, which creates the contrast with verse 15's eternal satisfaction.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲנִ֗י בְּ֭צֶדֶק אֶחֱזֶ֣ה פָנֶ֑יךָ אֶשְׂבְּעָ֥ה בְ֝הָקִ֗יץ תְּמוּנָתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: As for me, I will behold thy face in righteousness: I shall be satisfied, when I awake, with thy likeness.
verses.rendering:
As for me — in righteousness I will behold your face;
when I awake, I will be satisfied with your likeness.
verses.expanded_rendering: The final verse is the psalm's crown and the answer to everything that preceded it. The emphatic ani ('as for me') sets the psalmist apart from the wicked of verse 14 with their bellies and babies and surplus. be-tsedeq echezeh fanekha ('in righteousness I will behold your face') — the verb chazah ('to see, behold, envision') is the verb of prophetic vision, of penetrating sight. The psalmist will see God's face (panim) — the ultimate promise, reserved for the upright (Psalm 11:7). The condition is tsedeq ('righteousness'): only the righteous see God. The second line reaches further: esbe'ah be-haqits temunatekha ('I will be satisfied when I awake with your form/likeness'). The verb sava ('to be sated, satisfied to the full') is the same verb used for the wicked's satisfaction in verse 14 — but the objects are entirely different. They are satisfied with food and children; the psalmist will be satisfied with God's temunah ('form, likeness, image'). The phrase be-haqits ('when I awake') has been read as awakening from sleep, from the sleep of death, or from the long night of suffering. Whatever its primary reference, it reaches toward a morning in which the first thing the psalmist sees is God — and that seeing is enough.
translator_notes: temunah ('form, likeness') is the word used in Numbers 12:8 for what Moses sees when God speaks to him face to face — 'the form of the LORD.' It is also the word used in Deuteronomy 4:12, 15-16 to describe what Israel did not see at Sinai (and must not make into an image). The psalm promises the psalmist will see what Israel was denied and warned against replicating.

The tension between be-haqits ('when I awake') and the rest of the psalm's legal framework has generated enormous theological reflection. Does it refer to waking from literal sleep (the psalmist prays at night and expects to see God in the morning)? From the sleep of death (resurrection hope)? From the night of suffering (eschatological awakening)? The ambiguity may be the point — the psalm's language is capacious enough for all three readings.

key_terms.semantic_range: form, shape, likeness, image, appearance, representation
key_terms.note: temunah is what Moses saw (Numbers 12:8) and what Israel was forbidden to replicate in carved form (Deuteronomy 4:16). The word occupies the dangerous intersection between the visible and the invisible God. The psalmist claims that seeing God's temunah will be the source of ultimate satisfaction — not food, not wealth, not children, but the direct perception of God's form.
